title: Evaluating Tracker Performance
concept: Tracker quality is measured with metrics like MOTA (overall accuracy combining misses, false positives, and ID switches) and IDF1 (how well identities are preserved), giving engineers a way to compare tracking systems objectively.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What engineering fix best addresses frequent ID switches among shoppers walking closely together?
exercises.explain: Yes! Better appearance features help distinguish closely-spaced objects.

cards.title: How do you grade a tracker?
cards.body: If Tracker A misses a few detections but never swaps identities, and Tracker B catches every object but frequently swaps IDs during crossings, which is 'better'? We need standardized metrics to answer this fairly.

cards.title: MOTA and IDF1
cards.body: MOTA (Multi-Object Tracking Accuracy) combines missed detections, false positives, and ID switches into one overall accuracy score. IDF1 specifically measures how well object identities are preserved over the full track lifetime, weighting identity consistency more heavily.

cards.title: A report card with multiple subjects
cards.body: MOTA is like an overall GPA that blends several subjects (detection misses, false alarms, ID switches) into one number. IDF1 is like a single subject grade focused specifically on 'did you correctly remember who was who' — useful when identity consistency matters most for your application.

cards.title: Comparing two traffic trackers
cards.body: Tracker A: few missed cars, but frequent ID switches when cars pass each other (lower IDF1). Tracker B: occasionally misses a car in heavy rain, but almost never swaps IDs (higher IDF1). For a system computing individual car trajectories, Tracker B's higher IDF1 likely matters more than Tracker A's slightly better detection recall.

cards.title: A single number doesn't tell the whole story
cards.body: It's tempting to just chase the highest MOTA score. But MOTA blends several error types together, so two trackers with identical MOTA scores can have very different failure profiles (one has more ID switches, the other more missed detections) — always look at the breakdown, not just the summary number.

left.text:
Blends misses + false positives + ID switches
One overall accuracy number
Good for general tracker comparison

right.text:
Focuses specifically on identity consistency
Weighs long-term identity preservation
Good when 'who is who' matters most

exercises.prompt: What does MOTA combine into a single overall score?

options.text: Missed detections, false positives, and ID switches
Only the number of GPUs used for inference

exercises.explain: Correct! MOTA blends these three error types into one accuracy metric.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Two trackers with identical MOTA scores are guaranteed to have identical failure profiles (same mix of misses, false positives, ID switches).
exercises.explain: False. MOTA blends multiple error types together, so the same overall score can hide very different underlying failure patterns.

exercises.sentence: IDF1 specifically measures how well object ___ is preserved across the full track lifetime.
exercises.explain: IDF1 focuses on identity consistency, weighting it more heavily than a general accuracy blend.

exercises.prompt: Why might a system that computes individual car trajectories prefer a tracker with a higher IDF1 even if its overall MOTA is slightly lower?

options.text: Trajectory computation depends heavily on correct identity continuity, which IDF1 directly measures
IDF1 always predicts a car's exact speed automatically
MOTA cannot be computed for vehicle tracking at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Identity-sensitive applications benefit more from strong IDF1 performance.

exercises.prompt: We have completed our study of Multi-Object Tracking — detection-by-tracking, data association, real-world challenges, and evaluation metrics. What is the next logical computer vision application to study?

options.text: Image Retrieval & Similarity Search — finding visually similar images using learned embeddings
Restarting the curriculum from classical filtering
Deleting the entire tracking pipeline and starting over
Skipping directly to model deployment without further vision topics

exercises.explain: Exactly! Now that we can track objects across frames, let's learn how to find similar images across an entire database. Next up: Image Retrieval & Similarity Search!
